All reports
REDX

What’s Next Proposal

The defined work that turns the position into a system the market can see, buy, and trust.

Prepared by [Author - to be filled]
June 2026

0. The Commitment

The next phase builds the apparatus the Strategic Alignment Brief calls for: the operational fix that earns the right to claim accuracy, the proof system that makes the named-agent wins sell the next agent, the first-party evidence that the brand position currently rests on belief, and the facilitated sessions that turn the three open leadership questions into decisions. Each initiative below carries two ratings. Effort is how hard the work is. Confidence is a different axis: how sure we are this is the right next move, the conviction that it belongs in the next phase at all. It measures whether the move is worth making, a separate question from how strong the evidence behind the underlying call is. A move can be high-conviction even where the Brief’s evidence still leans, because fixing a lived customer problem or building proof is the right move regardless of how the open strategic calls resolve. Each card says what would raise its conviction.

1. Initiative Cards

Initiative 1. The data-hygiene fix

  1. Do this first. This is the only initiative that needs no leadership debate and no partner conversation. It needs a budget decision. Every accuracy claim, the parity-at-lower-cost campaign, and the credibility of the whole system-plus-coaching promise sit behind it.
  2. Why Now. REDX’s own named customers report bad, disconnected, and do-not-call numbers, and data accuracy is the single most dominant negative review theme across G2, BBB, and Trustindex (Brief, Alignment Point 2). You cannot reposition out of an experience customers are living, and a brand promise built on top of a broken data experience makes the dominant complaint read as a broken promise (Brief, Failure Mode 1).
  3. The Move. We de-dupe do-not-call, disconnected, and already-listed numbers, tighten the confidence-rating filter, set honest accuracy expectations at onboarding, and define the post-fix connect-rate benchmark versus Vulcan7 that gates the parity claim.
  4. Scope. A de-duplication and suppression pass against DNC, disconnected, and active-listing flags; a recalibrated confidence-rating filter; an onboarding accuracy-expectation screen and confidence-rating explainer; and a documented post-fix connect-rate benchmark that the parity-at-lower-cost campaign cannot launch without.
  5. Confidence this is the right next move. 5/5. A lived, named, multi-platform customer complaint gates four other moves; fixing it is correct under every resolution of the open strategic questions. Already at ceiling.

Effort. 4/5.

Sequence note. Depends on nothing. Gates Initiative 6 (the parity campaign cannot ship until the benchmark clears) and underwrites Initiative 4.

Initiative 2. The named-agent proof library

  1. Why Now. REDX publishes 16 named agents with quantified outcomes on its own case-study wall, Kayla Dieppa, Len Nevin, Cindy Strait, Scott Catton, Emily Peckham, Edward Estrada, and it is the rarest asset in the category, sitting underused on the public surface (Brief, Part 1 and Alignment Point 1). No rival shows comparable named-outcome proof. This asset is what converts the system-plus-coaching identity from a claim into a reason to buy.
  2. The Move. We turn the case-study wall into a structured, searchable proof system tied to product and agent type, and start the named-customer release-rights process so the library keeps growing.
  3. Scope. A tagged, filterable proof library (by product used, agent tenure, lead type, and outcome); a release-rights workflow run first against the 16 agents already published, whose stories need no new approval, then extended to new agents; and a set of proof modules the homepage, pricing page, and sales talk-tracks pull from so every claim anchors on a named agent rather than an adjective.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 5/5. The asset already exists and outperforms every rival’s; structuring it is high-conviction independent of how the brand-identity call resolves. What would raise it past where it sits: nothing on conviction; on impact, faster release-rights turnaround would widen the usable roster.

Effort. 3/5.

Sequence note. Runs in parallel with Initiative 1. Feeds the site-surface work in Initiative 5 and the on-ramp in Initiative 4. Carries the Failure Mode 4 mitigation: lead on the 16 already-published agents so a slow release-rights review never blocks the relaunch.

Initiative 3. The first-party evidence pass

  1. Why Now. This engagement has no first-party customer interviews. The brand-identity call, that customers buy and stay for the system plus coaching, rests on synthesized public reviews and leadership belief, and the Brief flags first-party win-and-retention data tying outcomes to coaching engagement as the thing that would settle it (Brief, Alignment Point 1). The pricing-ladder call has the same gap: there is no first-party price-objection data showing where buyers actually balk (Brief, Alignment Point 4).
  2. The Move. We run a primary-research pass of AI-led customer interviews across REDX’s own account base to confirm what drives purchase, retention, and price objection, and to harvest new named stories.
  3. Scope. A target set drawn from three account types, high-coaching-engagement winners, early churners, and price-sensitive new agents; a custom interview guide that tests the system-plus-coaching purchase driver, the retention link to coaching, and the price-objection point on the ladder; a findings memo that confirms or corrects the brand-identity and pricing calls; and a batch of fresh, release-cleared named stories that feed Initiative 2.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5. High-conviction because two open Brief calls turn on first-party evidence the company does not yet have, and the same pass produces both the answer and new proof assets. Held below 5 only because its priority sits behind the operational fix. What would raise it: leadership confirming the brand-identity shift is the decision they want this evidence to inform.

Effort. 3/5.

Sequence note. Depends on nothing operationally; can start alongside Initiative 1. Its findings inform the brand-identity session and the pricing session in Initiative 7, and its stories feed Initiative 2.

Initiative 4. The new and part-time agent on-ramp

  1. Why Now. Over 80% of new agents leave before their first renewal for lack of prospecting support, and the willing-but-priced-out new and part-time agent is shut out of the ~$2,000+/month team tech stack, inside a lead-gen software market growing at ~8.2% annually even as the agent base contracts (Brief, Alignment Point 5 and Appendix B). REDX already holds named new-agent proof here: Cindy Strait at 47 homes in a year, Edward Estrada at five listings in two days.
  2. The Move. We package the affordable single-product entry plus guided coaching into a defined on-ramp for the underserved willing-but-priced-out agent, with named new-agent proof front and center.
  3. Scope. A productized entry offer (the low-price single product laddering to the full system); an onboarding and coaching track built for the new and part-time agent; landing pages and talk-tracks anchored on Strait and Estrada; and a defined upward path from the on-ramp into the premium full system.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 3/5. This is the most contingent card. It is the best-evidenced near-term growth move and plays to proof REDX already has, but it commits toward the deepen-the-vertical side of an open growth question the Brief does not settle. What would raise it: the growth-direction decision in Initiative 7 landing on the in-vertical underserved segment, and the data-hygiene fix clearing so the on-ramp does not deliver a broken first experience.

Effort. 3/5.

Sequence note. Depends on Initiative 1 (a new agent must not hit bad data on day one) and on the growth-direction call teed up in Initiative 7. Draws proof from Initiative 2.

Initiative 5. The site-surface execution pass

  1. Why Now. The public site files education under “Resources,” leads with a data-and-feature checklist, carries the unaudited headline percentages, and frames the regulatory story on a premise the record contradicts (Brief, Alignment Points 1, 3, and 6). These are the Customer-Facing surfaces where the position the leadership team ratifies has to actually appear. Once the identity and claims decisions are made, the copy and information-architecture work is execution, and it is the work that makes the market see the position at all.
  2. The Move. We rewrite the homepage, primary navigation, pricing copy, and regulatory copy to carry the ratified system-plus-coaching identity, anchored on named-agent proof and the sourced 0.4-1.2% portal-conversion contrast.
  3. Scope. A homepage and navigation rebuild that moves education out of support and into the headline; pricing copy that presents the entry point and full system as one deliberate ladder; a claims-guardrail pass that removes the 44%, 29%, 3x, and 40-50% figures until audited and swaps “best data” for “parity at lower cost”; and regulatory copy reframed to the litigation-risk hedge with every “rules tightening” claim removed.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5. The surfaces must carry the position regardless of how the identity call’s final shape lands; the conviction is high because some version of this rewrite is needed under every resolution. What would raise it: leadership ratifying the system-plus-coaching identity so the rewrite executes the final position rather than a provisional one.

Effort. 3/5.

Sequence note. Depends on the brand-identity and regulatory-frame decisions in Initiative 7, and pulls proof modules from Initiative 2. The claims-guardrail portion can ship immediately; removing unaudited figures and correcting the regulatory premise are corrections that wait on no contingent call.

Initiative 6. The audited-figures and parity campaign

  1. Why Now. The headline figures REDX uses internally, 44% of expireds relist, 29% of FSBOs list, 40-50% conversion, cannot be quoted as audited, and a competitor or journalist who asks for the source finds none, which damages credibility with the sophisticated buyer the brand targets (Brief, Alignment Point 3 and Failure Mode 2). Vulcan7 holds a premium reputation built on branding, not structure, and its own pages cannot agree on their headline figure (Brief, Alignment Point 2).
  2. The Move. We commission the first-party analysis that produces auditable list-rate and conversion figures, then deploy the parity-at-lower-cost data campaign against Vulcan7 once the post-fix benchmark clears.
  3. Scope. A first-party analysis tying REDX lead types to documented list and conversion rates, producing audited figures that replace the fenced percentages; and a parity-at-lower-cost campaign, launched only after Initiative 1’s connect-rate benchmark clears, that challenges Vulcan7’s reputation-only premium on a credible data-parity claim at lower cost.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5. The audit is high-conviction: it either produces real numbers to use or confirms they cannot be claimed, and both outcomes protect the brand. The campaign half is gated on operational results, which is why it sits below 5. What would raise it: the audit returning defensible figures and Initiative 1’s benchmark showing the hygiene problem resolved.

Effort. 3/5.

Sequence note. The audit depends on nothing and can run alongside Initiative 3. The parity campaign depends on Initiative 1’s benchmark and follows the site-surface guardrail pass in Initiative 5.

Initiative 7. The leadership decision sessions

  1. Why Now. The Brief leaves three calls open by design, because the evidence does not settle them: where to grow (deepen the vertical or leap beyond real estate), how to frame the regulatory story (litigation-risk hedge or superior system regardless of regulation), and whether to commit the full brand to system-plus-coaching and demote “best data” (Brief, “What you need to discuss and decide”). These belong to leadership, and the firm’s job is to bring each to a clean decision rather than pre-decide it. They also gate the execution initiatives above.
  2. The Move. We facilitate three working sessions that bring the evidence, the open question, and the tradeoff to the table and drive each to a committed decision.
  3. Scope. A brand-identity session (commit to leading on system-plus-coaching and demoting “best data,” or not), informed by the Initiative 3 findings; a growth-direction session that scopes and reads the funded non-real-estate test against the cost of capturing the in-vertical segment, then commits to one direction rather than funding both half-way; and a regulatory-frame session that ratifies the corrected litigation-risk-hedge premise and chooses how multi-channel is positioned. Each session ends in a recorded decision and an owner.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5. High-conviction that these decisions must be forced rather than left to drift, which is itself a named failure mode (Brief, Failure Mode 3). Below 5 only because the growth-direction session depends on a funded test that takes setup. What would raise it: the non-real-estate proof test being scoped and funded so the growth session reads measured payback figures instead of leadership instinct.

Effort. 2/5.

Sequence note. The brand-identity session depends on Initiative 3’s findings and unblocks Initiative 5. The growth-direction session depends on a funded non-real-estate test and unblocks Initiative 4. The regulatory session depends on nothing and can run first.

2. Sequencing

The order runs by dependency, not by calendar.

Start the data-hygiene fix (Initiative 1) now; it gates the most downstream work and needs only a budget decision. In parallel, three initiatives that depend on nothing can begin at once: the proof-library build (Initiative 2), the first-party evidence pass (Initiative 3), and the audit half of Initiative 6. The regulatory-frame session and the claims-guardrail copy can also ship immediately, because both are corrections rather than contingent calls.

Once the first-party evidence pass returns findings, the brand-identity session (Initiative 7) can resolve, which unblocks the full site-surface rewrite (Initiative 5). Once the growth-direction test is scoped and funded, the growth session resolves, which unblocks the new-agent on-ramp (Initiative 4). The parity campaign (Initiative 6) waits on one named condition: the post-fix connect-rate benchmark from Initiative 1 clearing.

The data fix and the open-question sessions are the two pacing items; everything client-facing waits on one or the other.

3. What We Need From You

Each initiative needs a specific input or decision from the leadership team to start or to finish.

  • Initiative 1 (data-hygiene fix). A budget approval and a named operations owner. No strategic decision required.
  • Initiative 2 (proof library). Sign-off to begin the release-rights process and a marketing owner for the library. The 16 published agents need no new approval.
  • Initiative 3 (first-party evidence pass). Account-base access and confirmation that the brand-identity shift is the decision this evidence is meant to inform.
  • Initiative 4 (new-agent on-ramp). The growth-direction decision from the Initiative 7 session, and confirmation the data fix has cleared.
  • Initiative 5 (site-surface rewrite). The ratified brand-identity and regulatory-frame decisions. The claims-guardrail removals can proceed on your approval alone.
  • Initiative 6 (audited figures and parity campaign). Access to first-party list-rate data for the audit, and the cleared connect-rate benchmark before the campaign launches.
  • Initiative 7 (decision sessions). Leadership attendance and a commitment to leave each session with a recorded decision and an owner, including the funded scope for the non-real-estate proof test.
